coredata.dc:title: Specific electron-transfer and surface plasmon resonance integrated boosting visible-light photoelectrochemical sensor for 4-chlorophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Can
Zhao, Yu
Xu, Li
Yan, Pengcheng
Qian, Junchao
Zhao, Long
Zhang, Jianming
Li, Henan
coredata.dc:description: Emerging analytical technologies are being developed to provide advanced methods for monitoring 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) in the environment. Herein, a label-free, sensitive, and rapid photoelectrochemical (PEC) 4-CP detection system was constructed based on ternary composites of Ag nanoparticles, graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) and carbon spheres (CS) (Ag/GCN/CS). The proposed ternary composites were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. In this process, ascorbic acid (AA) played a crucial role in reducing Ag+ to metallic Ag, and provided a carbon source for CS. Due to synergistic promotion by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer behavior of CS, the ternary composites exhibited a broad visible light response and fast charge transfer, leading to a tremendously enhanced PEC response. Based on the promotion of the PEC response, a PEC sensor for 4-CP was designed by detecting the photocurrent signals of the PEC electrode after adding 4-CP to solution. Under optimal conditions, the PEC sensor exhibited a wide linear range for 4-CP from 16 to 1104ngmL−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.33ngmL−1. In particular, the present work may assistance in better understanding the synergistic effect between the SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer properties of CS. The results can also be applied to other PEC and photocatalytic systems in conjunction with high-performance photoactive materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ternary composite
PEC sensor
SPR
Electron-transfer
Carbon materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported under the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705058 ), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. BK20170524 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018T110450 ), the Research Foundation of Jiangsu University ( 17JDG007 ), and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image and (b) TEM image of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites; (c) XRD spectra and (d) FT-IR of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites and GCN, respectively. (The inset: magnified XRD pattern of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites.)
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) PL of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites and GCN, respectively.

(a) Nyquist plots of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO and GCN/ITO in 0.1molL−1 KCl solution containing 3mmolL−1 [Ru(NH3)6 3+]Cl3/[Ru(NH3)6 2+]Cl2 (1:1) mixture in the frequency range from 0.01Hz to 10kHz and (b) photocurrent responses of GCN/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO and Ag/GCN/CS/ITO in phosphate buffer solution (0.1molL−1, pH7.0).

(a) Photocurrent responses of the Ag/GCN/CS/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO and GCN/ITO in the absence (b, d, f) and presence (a, c, e) of 200ngmL−1 4-CP; (b) stable photocurrent responses curve of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO in the presence of 200ngmL−1 4-CP; (c) photocurrent responses at Ag/GCN/CS/ITO towards 4-CP at increasing concentrations; (d) the linear calibration curve for 4-CP detection.
Influence of interfering substances on the responses of PEC sensor for 200ngmL−1 4-CP.
The illustration of the plausible PEC mechanism of the Ag/GCN/CS/ITO in spectral regions of (a) 420–550nm and (b) 550–800nm.

head.ce:title: Specific electron-transfer and surface plasmon resonance integrated boosting visible-light photoelectrochemical sensor for 4-chlorophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Environment Functional Materials, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Environment Functional Materials
School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging analytical technologies are being developed to provide advanced methods for monitoring 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) in the environment. Herein, a label-free, sensitive, and rapid photoelectrochemical (PEC) 4-CP detection system was constructed based on ternary composites of Ag nanoparticles, graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) and carbon spheres (CS) (Ag/GCN/CS). The proposed ternary composites were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. In this process, ascorbic acid (AA) played a crucial role in reducing Ag+ to metallic Ag, and provided a carbon source for CS. Due to synergistic promotion by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer behavior of CS, the ternary composites exhibited a broad visible light response and fast charge transfer, leading to a tremendously enhanced PEC response. Based on the promotion of the PEC response, a PEC sensor for 4-CP was designed by detecting the photocurrent signals of the PEC electrode after adding 4-CP to solution. Under optimal conditions, the PEC sensor exhibited a wide linear range for 4-CP from 16 to 1104ngmL−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.33ngmL−1. In particular, the present work may assistance in better understanding the synergistic effect between the SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer properties of CS. The results can also be applied to other PEC and photocatalytic systems in conjunction with high-performance photoactive materials.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-Chlorophenol (4-CP) has been widely applied as a kind of industrial raw material in applications such as mineral refining, pesticide production and dye synthesis [1–5]. However, 4-CP has been considered to be a poisonous reagent due to its carcinogenicity to human beings [2,5]. Furthermore, 4-CP has a low biodegradability, which causes the compound to persist in nature and results in lasting damage [2]. Thus, developing sensitive and accurate detection methods for 4-CP in water bodies has attracted widespread interest with regard to environmental protection and human health. Traditional techniques for detecting the presence of 4-CP, include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fluorescence, liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS), gas chromatography with mass spectrometry (GC–MS) and electrochemical sensing [6–9]. Although these methods show satisfactory sensitivity and accuracy, the cumbersome operation, complex preprocessing and high-cost equipment required obstruct the development of these detection techniques. Therefore, developing a low-cost, sensitive, and accurate method for detecting 4-CP is an urgent priority.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors are becoming more and more promising in the field of detection due to their low background signal and high sensitivity [10–14]. Due to these excellent properties, PEC sensors are often used to detect trace contaminants in the environment and even to detect specific chemical substances in cells to identify the presence of cancer cells [15–17]. Suitable photoactive materials play a vital role in promoting the development of PEC sensors [10]. Graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) is a good representative, because of its enduring chemical stability, easy preparation, lack of toxicity and unique photoactivity [18–21]. GCN has an appropriate band gap [19,20,22], which leads to excellent performance in photocatalysis and photoelectrochemistry [19]. However, GCN has low electrical conductivity and high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, which can limit the PEC performance of GCN. To date, doping carbon materials and noble metals with GCN has been considered to be the most effective way to upgrade the electrical conductivity of GCN and improve the transfer and rapid separation of photogenerated carriers [22,23]. For instance, Au/graphene oxide/GCN composites have been fabricated, and the products exhibited an obvious improvement in photocatalytic activity [23].

Ascorbic acid (AA), also as known as vitamin C, is applied in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Currently, AA has is used as a source of biomass in the production of carbon materials. AA can not only provide a source of carbon structure but also participate in reaction processes as a reducing agent [24]. Herein, we report the synthesis of ternary composites of Ag nanoparticles, graphitic carbon nitride (GCN), and carbon spheres (CS) (Ag/GCN/CS), and reveal that the as-proposed Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites exhibited excellent PEC performance. In particular, these ternary composites showed a higher PEC response in PEC testing compared with that of pure GCN, which may be attributed to the synergistic effect between the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer behavior of CS [25–27]. Especially, Ag nanoparticles may play a chief role in the charge transfer processes due to the wavelength-dependent dual role as the electron sink and hot-electron donor. The incident light can be divided into two domains, 420–550nm and 550–800nm, as considering the absorption edge of pure GCN (~550nm). In the wavelength region of 420–550nm, Ag nanoparticles as the electron sink can trap the electrons produced by the excited GCN, thus suppressing the electron-hole recombination. Upon irradiation light of 550–800nm, the SPR effect causes a strong oscillation of electrons on the surface of the Ag nanoparticles and leads to hot electron transfer from the surface of the Ag nanoparticles to the conduction band (CB) of GCN [28–31]. This transfer can efficiently reduce the recombination rate of electrons and holes. Additionally, CS, functioning as excellent electronic conductors, can promote the transfer of electrons. Such synergistic effect eventually promotes the generation and separation of photogenerated carriers. The holes on the surface of the GCN and/or Ag nanoparticles can be sacrificed with the addition of 4-CP, leading to an even further enhancement of the photocurrent intensity. Based on the photocurrent signal response, a PEC sensor for 4-CP was constructed from the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites. This PEC sensor exhibited acceptable sensitivity, reproducibility, specificity and stability.

Cyanuric chloride, melamine, acetonitrile, ethanol, dibasic sodium phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, 4-chlorophenol, silver nitrate, sodium hydroxide, ascorbic acid and sodium citrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The indium tin oxide (ITO) (sheet resistance <7Ωsq.− 1) slices were obtained from Zhuhai Kaivo Electronic Components Co., Ltd. (China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (18MΩcm−1, Milli-Q, Millipore).

The crystalline phase of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites was tested by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 diffractometer) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418Å). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed on a Nicolet spectrophotometer (Nexus 470) using the standard KBr. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) measurements were taken with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL JSM-7001F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated at an acceleration voltage of 10kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2010). Diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was performed on a UV-3600 Plus in the range of 200–800nm using BaSO4 as the reflectance standard material. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. The testing light was a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300/300UV) with a cutoff filter (λ: 420–2500nm). The average light intensity is ~40mWcm−2. The monochromatic light measurements were performed with high power light-emitting diode (LED) light sources (PLS-LED100B) with 450nm (blue light, 68.1mWcm−2) and 630nm (red light, 64.1mWcm−2), respectively.

All of the PEC testing was carried out on a CHI660B electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode system, including a Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and modified ITO as the working electrode, at a constant potential of −0.2V (vs. Ag/AgCl) under a nitrogen atmosphere in phosphate buffer solution (0.1molL−1, pH7.0). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a 0.1molL−1 KCl solution containing a 3mmolL−1 [Ru(NH3)6 3+]Cl3/[Ru(NH3)6 2+]Cl2 (1:1) mixture in the frequency range from 0.01Hz to 10kHz.

Cyanuric chloride (5mmol) was added to acetonitrile (20mL) and stirred continuously until completely dissolved. Next, melamine (2.5mmol) was slowly added to the resulting solution slowly. The intermediate was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 48h at 160°C. The final mixture was cooled to room temperature and washed with water and ethanol three times. After that, the final product was dried for further experiments.

GCN (0.02g) was added to a flask containing water (50mL) and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 1h. Then, AgNO3 solution (350μL, 10mgmL−1) was dropped into the above solution and stirred continuously for 30min at room temperature. After that, the obtained suspension was stirred continuously in an oil bath at 120°C. Then, AA solution (25.4μL, 10mgmL−1) was added rapidly to the solution. The mixture was stirred at 120°C for 1h. The final product was washed with water and ethanol three times and dried at 60°C for 12h before further use. In addition, Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites with different contents of Ag were prepared via the same method for comparative experiments. Besides, the composites without CS (Ag/GCN) were synthesized with sodium citrate instead of AA.

The ITO electrodes were ultrasonically cleaned in 2molL−1 of NaOH solution for 15min and purged with nitrogen and dried for subsequent use. Then, 2mg Ag/GCN/CS ternary composite was dispersed in 2mL deionized water to form a suspension (1mgmL−1). A total of 50μL of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composite (1mgmL−1) suspension was dropped evenly onto an ITO slice with an area of 0.5cm2. After drying, 20μL of Nafion solution (0.05wt%) was cast on the surface of the ITO electrode and then dried to form a Ag/GCN/CS ternary composite-modified ITO electrode (Ag/GCN/CS/ITO). In addition, GCN/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO and Ag/GCN/CS/ITO with different contents of Ag were prepared with the same method for comparison.

The morphology of the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites was investigated by SEM as shown in Fig. 1a. CS sintered from AA with an average diameter of 1.2μm were uniformly and firmly embedded in the layer structure of GCN. Ag nanoparticles (displayed as white spots) were dispersed in these ternary composites evenly. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) indicates that the mean size of the Ag nanoparticles was approximately 100nm. As the EDS results for the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites show (Fig. S1 and Table S1), the content of Ag in the as-prepared ternary composites was 10.66wt%. Moreover, the phase structure of the as-prepared Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites was analysed by XRD. In Fig. 1c, the GCN shows a diffraction peak at approximately 27.8°, corresponding to stacking along of (002) planes of the conjugated aromatic system; this peak demonstrates the existence of graphite-like structures in GCN. In the XRD pattern of the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites, peaks at 38.1, 44.3, 64.4, and 77.5° can be visually observed, corresponding to the typical major diffraction peaks of Ag (JCPDS NO. 65-2871). This result indicates that the Ag+ was successfully reduced to Ag nanoparticles by AA. As the inset in Fig. 1c shows, a weak peak near 27.8°, corresponding to the major diffraction peak of GCN, can be observed in the magnified image. Furthermore, no other peaks can be observed in the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites, revealing that the resulting sample exhibited high purity. To further investigate the composition of the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites, the functional groups of the as-prepared sample were detected by FT-IR spectroscopy. As Fig. 1d shows, the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites preserved a chemical structure similar to that of GCN, which suggests that Ag nanoparticles and CS did not change the structure of GCN. Broad peaks corresponding to NH stretching were observed between 3400 and 3000cm−1, suggesting that some of the nitrogen atoms were hydrogenated [32,33]. A series of consecutive peaks appeared between 1800 and 900cm−1, which can be characterized as bridging C-NH-C and trigonal C-N(C)-C units [33]. These results can show successful synthesis of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites.

DRS was used to evaluate the optical absorption properties of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites. As Fig. 2a shows, pure GCN exhibited broad absorption with an absorption edge at approximately 550nm. Compared with GCN, the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites possessed an obvious red-shift within the visible-light range and better optical absorption capability. After the Ag nanoparticles were introduced, an obvious absorption band was observed in the visible region between 500 and 600nm, indicating the SPR characteristic of Ag nanoparticles [34]. These results indicate that the doping of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of GCN can enhance the optional absorption performance of GCN. According to UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, a Tauc plot was calculated to obtain the band gap of the GCN (Fig. S2). The band gap energy (E g) for the GCN was determined to 2.45eV. The conduction band minimum (CBM) value of the GCN was examined by the cathodic scan result, and thus the CBM was measured to be −1.05eV (vs normal hydrogen electrode (NHE)) [35]. According to the equation of E CB = E VB − E g, the corresponding valence band maximum (VBM) was obtained to be 1.40eV [36]. PL spectra were chosen to investigate the recombination status of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Pure GCN showed an emission band at 420–490nm (Fig. 2b). The Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites exhibited a similar peak shape and position, but the emission intensity of the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites was far weaker than that of GCN, indicating that the doping of Ag nanoparticles and CS on the surface of GCN suppressed the recombination process of photogenerated electron-hole pairs effectively, resulting in the enhanced PEC performance of the Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites [19,37].

The charge transfer capability was investigated by EIS. Theoretically, a smaller arc radius means that a material has a higher charge transfer rate [11]. As shown in Fig. 3a, the radius of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO was much smaller than that of Ag/GCN/ITO and the radius of Ag/GCN/ITO was smaller than that of GCN/ITO. This phenomenon demonstrates that Ag/GCN/CS/ITO possessed a higher charge transfer rate and better electrical conductivity than that of Ag/GCN/ITO and GCN/ITO. The photocurrent responses of GCN/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO, and Ag/GCN/CS/ITO are shown in Fig. 3b. Each modified unit, once the light was turned on, had an evident increased photocurrent. When the excited light disappeared, the photocurrent response instantly returned to the background value. The photocurrent response measured on Ag/GCN/CS/ITO was almost 2.5 times higher than that of GCN/ITO and almost 1.9 times higher than that of Ag/GCN/ITO, demonstrating that the synergistic effect between Ag nanoparticles and CS promoted the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer rate of electrons, resulting in the enhanced PEC performance of Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites.

The PEC properties of as-prepared Ag/GCN/CS/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO and GCN/ITO were evaluated by adding 4-CP and observing the photocurrent changes under the excited light. As shown in Fig. 4a, GCN/ITO showed a photocurrent of 0.42μAcm−2 (curve a). Compared with GCN/ITO, the Ag/GCN/ITO displayed a stronger photocurrent of 0.62μAcm−2 (curve c), and the Ag/GCN/CS/ITO displayed a strongest photocurrent of 1.10μAcm−2 (curve e). 4-CP (200ngmL−1) was added to the testing system, resulting in an increased photocurrent response. The photocurrent of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO increased by 0.34μAcm−2 (curve e, f), which was 5.7 times higher than that of the photocurrent increment of the GCN/ITO (curve a, b) and 1.7 times higher than that of the photocurrent increment of the Ag/GCN/ITO (curve c, d). Although the exact mechanism is not quite fully clarified, according to previous research and the current study, the greatly enhanced photocurrent upon Ag/GCN/CS/ITO could be mainly ascribed to the wavelength-dependent dual role of the Ag nanoparticles as the electron sink and hot-electron donor (Scheme 1 ) [28–31]. In our study, the incident light can be divided into two spectral regions, 420–550nm and 550–800nm, taken the absorption edge of pure GCN (~550nm) as the reference. In the light region of 420–550nm electron-hole pairs are generated from excited GCN, in which the electrons can be trapped by Ag nanoparticles from the CB of GCN. This electron sink function of Ag nanoparticles is of high benefit to suppress the electron-hole recombination, and the efficient charge separation leads to an enhanced PEC current, as illustrated in Scheme 1a. Upon irradiation light of 550–800nm, a strong near field is created at the interface of Ag-GCN due to the SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles, resulting in the generation of hot electrons. The interfacial near field further promotes the injection of hot electrons from Ag nanoparticles to the CB of GCN, accomplishing the charge separation, as illustrated in Scheme 1b. In addition, the introduction of CS, an efficient electron acceptor and transporter, largely accelerates the transfer of electrons to the external circuit in all the above processes. As a result, the synergistic effect between the SPR of Ag nanoparticles and electron-transfer behavior of CS significantly improves the charge separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and largely enhances the PEC response.

We performed the measurement of photo responses of GCN/ITO, Ag/GCN/ITO, and Ag/GCN/CS/ITO to verify the beneficial role of Ag nanoparticles in the enhanced PEC response under irradiation of two kinds of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with the wavelength of 450nm (blue light) and 630nm (red light). As shown in Fig. S3a, only a relatively low photocurrent of the GCN/ITO can be observed under blue light. By stark contrast, the photocurrent density of Ag/GCN/ITO is ~3.4 times higher than that of GCN/ITO, owing to the introduction of Ag nanoparticles which strongly suppresses the electron-hole recombination of GCN. As illustrated in Fig. S3b, the photo response of GCN/ITO is negligible, because GCN hardly absorbs red light as presented in its absorption spectrum. Whereas, for the case of Ag/GCN/ITO under red LED illumination, a significant enhancement of the photocurrent is quite visible, which can be mainly attributed to the SPR induced hot electron generation of Ag nanoparticles upon the absorption of red light. In addition, the introduction of CS, an excellent conducting material, further enhanced the photocurrent response, leading to the superior photoresponsibility of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO under the illumination of both wavelengths. Therefore, these results strongly support the mechanism proposed above. Simultaneously, the hole scavenger 4-CP, sacrifices the generated holes on the surface of GCN and/or Ag nanoparticles, and thus inhibiting the electron-hole recombination. In consider of the wide spectral range in current study, it is believed that the increased PEC response of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO as compared to GCN/ITO is the consequence of the interplay of all charge transfer processes at the interface. In addition, the stability of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO was examined. Fig. 4b shows the photocurrent response curve of Ag/GCN/C/ITO in phosphate buffer solution (0.1molL−1, pH7.0) containing 200ngmL−1 of 4-CP. The photocurrent responses of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO were repeatedly tested 12 times at 20s intervals, and no obvious change was observed. The results indicate that the photocurrent intensity of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO showed an acceptable stability, which was beneficial for the fabrication of a PEC sensor in the detection of 4-CP pollutants.

Due to the enhancement of the PEC response of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO by 4-CP, a 4-CP sensitive detection system was successfully developed. The photocurrent response of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO with various concentrations of 4-CP is shown in Fig. 4c. The photocurrent intensities of Ag/GCN/CS/ITO had a rising trend with increased concentrations of 4-CP. By investigating the relation between the photocurrent intensities and the concentrations of 4-CP, an excellent linear relationship was obtained (I is the photocurrent after adding 4-CP). As Fig. 4d shows, a linear portion was easily identified at 4-CP concentrations ranging from 16 to 1104ngmL−1. The linearization equation was I =3.53×10−3 C4-CP +0.489 (R2 =0.998). The limit of detection (LOD) for 4-CP was 5.33ngmL−1 with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. Compared with previous methods for detecting 4-CP (Table S2), this PEC sensor showed a lower detection limit. The result demonstrated that the 4-CP PEC sensor exhibited a low detection limit and can be applied to the sensitive monitoring of 4-CP.

To investigate the selectivity of the as-proposed PEC sensor, the effects of common metal ions and organics on the detection of 4-CP with Ag/GCN/CS/ITO were carefully examined. As shown in Fig. 5 , K2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, hydroquinone (HQ), floxacin and enrofloxacin at a concentration of 10μgmL−1, approximately 50 times that of 4-CP, were measured to investigate the sensor responses. The results suggest that the effect of various compounds on 4-CP detection was negligible and the sensor had a distinct specificity. The reproducibility of the 4-CP PEC sensor was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4, 200ngmL−1 4-CP solution was repeatedly analysed using the same PEC sensor five times, indicating an intraassay relative standard deviation (RSD) of below 5%. The results suggest that the fabricated 4-CP PEC sensor possessed an acceptable reproducibility for 4-CP detection.

In summary, Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites were synthesized by the method of a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis. AA played a crucial role in reducing Ag+ to Ag nanoparticles and providing a carbon source for the CS. Ag/GCN/CS/ITO exhibited a higher photocurrent response than that of pure GCN, which indicates that the synergistic effect of Ag nanoparticles and CS enhanced the charge transfer of GCN, leading to excellent PEC performance. A PEC sensor for 4-CP was fabricated based on Ag/GCN/CS ternary composites. The sensor showed a linear relationship between the photocurrent response and concentration of 4-CP in the range of 16 to 1104ngmL−1 with a LOD of 5.33ngmL−1. This PEC sensor platform with rapid response and simple operation can provide a potential technique for monitoring 4-CP in the practical environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported under the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21705058), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. BK20170524), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018T110450), the Research Foundation of Jiangsu University (17JDG007), and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.12.001.
